info.title: Authentication API
info.description:
# Authentication API

The Authentication API enables you to manage all aspects of user identity when you use Auth0. It offers endpoints so your users can log in, sign up, log out, access APIs, and more.

The API supports various identity protocols, like [OpenID Connect](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/protocols/openid-connect-protocol), [OAuth 2.0](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/protocols/oauth), [FAPI](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/highly-regulated-identity#advanced-security-with-openid-connect-fapi-), and [SAML](https://auth0.com/docs/protocols/saml).

**Note:** This API is designed for developers who are comfortable integrating with RESTful APIs. If you prefer a more guided approach, check out our [Quickstarts](https://auth0.com/docs/quickstarts) or [Libraries](https://auth0.com/docs/libraries).

## Base URL

The Authentication API is served over HTTPS. All URLs referenced in the documentation have the following base:

```
https://{yourDomain}
```

Replace `{yourDomain}` with your Auth0 tenant domain (e.g., `your-tenant.auth0.com` or `your-tenant.us.auth0.com`).

---

## Understanding OAuth 2.0 Flows

Auth0 implements various OAuth 2.0 flows to accommodate different application types and security requirements. The two most important endpoints for these flows are:

- **`/authorize`** - Initiates the authorization process
- **`/oauth/token`** - Exchanges credentials for tokens

### Flow Selection Guide

| Application Type | Recommended Flow | Endpoints Used |
|-----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Regular Web Application** (server-side) | Authorization Code Flow | `/authorize` → `/oauth/token` |
| **Single-Page Application** (SPA) | Authorization Code Flow with PKCE | `/authorize` → `/oauth/token` |
| **Native Mobile App** | Authorization Code Flow with PKCE | `/authorize` → `/oauth/token` |
| **Machine-to-Machine** (API, CLI, Daemon) | Client Credentials Flow | `/oauth/token` only |
| **Trusted First-Party App** | Resource Owner Password Flow | `/oauth/token` only |
| **Input-Constrained Device** (TV, IoT) | Device Authorization Flow | `/oauth/device/code` → `/oauth/token` |

---

## Common Authentication Flows

### 1. Authorization Code Flow

**Best for:** Server-side web applications that can securely store a client secret.

**How it works:**

1. **User initiates login** - Your app redirects the user to Auth0's `/authorize` endpoint:
   ```
   GET https://{yourDomain}/authorize?
     response_type=code&
     client_id={yourClientId}&
     redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}&
     scope=openid%20profile%20email&
     state={randomState}
   ```

2. **User authenticates** - Auth0 shows the login page, user enters credentials.

3. **Auth0 redirects back** - After successful authentication, Auth0 redirects to your `redirect_uri` with an authorization code:
   ```
   {yourCallbackUrl}?code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE&state={randomState}
   ```

4. **Exchange code for tokens** - Your backend exchanges the code for tokens at `/oauth/token`:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=authorization_code&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE&
   redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}
   ```

5. **Receive tokens** - Auth0 returns access token, ID token, and optionally a refresh token.

**Learn more:** [Authorization Code Flow](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/authorization-code-flow)

---

### 2. Authorization Code Flow with PKCE

**Best for:** Single-page applications (SPAs) and native mobile apps that cannot securely store a client secret.

**How it works:**

1. **Generate code verifier and challenge** - Your app generates:
   - A random `code_verifier` (cryptographically random string)
   - A `code_challenge` derived from the verifier: `BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier))`

2. **User initiates login** - Your app redirects to `/authorize` with the code challenge:
   ```
   GET https://{yourDomain}/authorize?
     response_type=code&
     client_id={yourClientId}&
     redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}&
     scope=openid%20profile%20email&
     state={randomState}&
     code_challenge={codeChallenge}&
     code_challenge_method=S256
   ```

3. **User authenticates** - Auth0 shows login page, user authenticates.

4. **Auth0 redirects back** - After authentication, Auth0 redirects with authorization code:
   ```
   {yourCallbackUrl}?code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE&state={randomState}
   ```

5. **Exchange code for tokens** - Your app exchanges the code using the original verifier:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=authorization_code&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE&
   code_verifier={codeVerifier}&
   redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}
   ```
   Note: No `client_secret` required!

6. **Receive tokens** - Auth0 returns tokens after verifying the code verifier.

**Why PKCE?** PKCE prevents authorization code interception attacks by ensuring only the app that initiated the flow can exchange the code for tokens.

**Learn more:** [Authorization Code Flow with PKCE](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/authorization-code-flow-with-pkce)

---

### 3. Implicit Flow (Deprecated)

**Status:** ⚠️ **Not recommended for new applications**

**Best for:** Legacy SPAs (replaced by Authorization Code Flow with PKCE).

**How it works:**

1. **User initiates login** - Your app redirects to `/authorize` requesting tokens directly:
   ```
   GET https://{yourDomain}/authorize?
     response_type=token&
     client_id={yourClientId}&
     redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}&
     scope=openid%20profile%20email&
     state={randomState}&
     nonce={randomNonce}
   ```

2. **Receive tokens immediately** - Auth0 redirects back with tokens in the URL fragment:
   ```
   {yourCallbackUrl}#access_token=...&id_token=...&token_type=Bearer&expires_in=86400
   ```

**Why deprecated?** Tokens are exposed in the browser history and cannot be securely stored. Use PKCE instead.

**Learn more:** [Implicit Flow](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/implicit-flow-with-form-post)

---

### 4. Client Credentials Flow

**Best for:** Machine-to-machine (M2M) communication where no user is involved.

**How it works:**

1. **Request token** - Your service authenticates with its credentials:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=client_credentials&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   audience={apiIdentifier}
   ```

2. **Receive access token** - Auth0 returns an access token for the API:
   ```json
   {
     "access_token": "eyJ...",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 86400
   }
   ```

3. **Call API** - Use the access token to call your API.

**Use cases:** Background jobs, microservices, CLIs, server-to-server integrations.

**Learn more:** [Client Credentials Flow](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/client-credentials-flow)

---

### 5. Resource Owner Password Flow

**Best for:** Highly trusted first-party applications (use sparingly).

**Status:** ⚠️ **Only use if redirect-based flows are not possible**

**How it works:**

1. **Collect credentials** - Your app collects username and password directly from the user.

2. **Request token** - Your app sends credentials to Auth0:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=password&
   username={username}&
   password={password}&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   audience={apiIdentifier}&
   scope=openid%20profile%20email
   ```

3. **Receive tokens** - Auth0 returns tokens if credentials are valid.

**Important:** This flow requires your app to handle user credentials directly. Use only when absolutely necessary.

**Learn more:** [Resource Owner Password Flow](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/resource-owner-password-flow)

---

### 6. Device Authorization Flow

**Best for:** Input-constrained devices like smart TVs, IoT devices, or gaming consoles.

**How it works:**

1. **Request device code** - Your device requests a code:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/device/code
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   client_id={yourClientId}&
   scope=openid%20profile&
   audience={apiIdentifier}
   ```

2. **Receive codes** - Auth0 returns:
   ```json
   {
     "device_code": "GmRh...",
     "user_code": "WDJB-MJHT",
     "verification_uri": "https://{yourDomain}/activate",
     "verification_uri_complete": "https://{yourDomain}/activate?user_code=WDJB-MJHT",
     "expires_in": 900,
     "interval": 5
   }
   ```

3. **Show user code** - Display the `user_code` and `verification_uri` to the user.

4. **User activates on another device** - User visits the URL on their phone/computer and enters the code.

5. **Poll for token** - Your device polls `/oauth/token` every `interval` seconds:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code&
   device_code={deviceCode}&
   client_id={yourClientId}
   ```

6. **Receive tokens** - Once user authorizes, Auth0 returns tokens.

**Learn more:** [Device Authorization Flow](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/device-authorization-flow)

---

### 7. Refresh Token Flow

**Purpose:** Obtain new access tokens without user interaction.

**How it works:**

1. **Use refresh token** - When your access token expires, request a new one:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=refresh_token&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   refresh_token={refreshToken}
   ```

2. **Receive new access token** - Auth0 returns a fresh access token:
   ```json
   {
     "access_token": "eyJ...",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 86400
   }
   ```

**Getting refresh tokens:** Include `offline_access` in the `scope` parameter during initial authorization.

**Learn more:** [Refresh Tokens](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/tokens/refresh-tokens)

---

## Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Flows

When MFA is enabled, authentication flows may require additional verification steps.

### MFA Flow Sequence

1. **Initial authentication** - User provides primary credentials (username/password).

2. **MFA required response** - Auth0 returns an `mfa_required` error with an `mfa_token`:
   ```json
   {
     "error": "mfa_required",
     "error_description": "Multifactor authentication required",
     "mfa_token": "Fe26...Ha"
   }
   ```

3. **Request MFA challenge** - Request a challenge using `/mfa/challenge`:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/mfa/challenge
   Content-Type: application/json

   {
     "mfa_token": "Fe26...Ha",
     "client_id": "{yourClientId}",
     "client_secret": "{yourClientSecret}",
     "challenge_type": "otp"
   }
   ```

4. **Complete MFA verification** - Submit the MFA code to `/oauth/token`:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/mfa-otp&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   mfa_token={mfaToken}&
   otp={otpCode}
   ```

5. **Receive tokens** - After successful MFA verification, Auth0 returns tokens.

### MFA Challenge Types

- **OTP (One-Time Password)** - Authenticator app codes (Google Authenticator, Authy, etc.)
- **OOB (Out-of-Band)** - SMS, voice call, or push notification
- **Recovery Code** - Backup codes for account recovery

**Learn more:** [Multi-factor Authentication](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/multi-factor-authentication)

---

## Passwordless Authentication Flows

Passwordless authentication allows users to log in without passwords using email or SMS.

### Passwordless Flow Sequence

1. **Start passwordless** - Request a verification code or magic link:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/passwordless/start
   Content-Type: application/json

   {
     "client_id": "{yourClientId}",
     "client_secret": "{yourClientSecret}",
     "connection": "email",
     "email": "user@example.com",
     "send": "code"
   }
   ```

2. **User receives code** - Auth0 sends a verification code via email or SMS.

3. **Verify and get tokens** - Exchange the code for tokens:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   grant_type=http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/passwordless/otp&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   username={emailOrPhone}&
   otp={verificationCode}&
   realm={connection}&
   scope=openid%20profile%20email
   ```

4. **Receive tokens** - Auth0 returns tokens after successful verification.

**Learn more:** [Passwordless Authentication](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/passwordless)

---

## Advanced Flows

### Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR)

**Best for:** High-security applications requiring FAPI compliance.

**How it works:**

1. **Push authorization parameters** - Send authorization request to `/oauth/par`:
   ```
   POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/par
   Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

   response_type=code&
   client_id={yourClientId}&
   client_secret={yourClientSecret}&
   redirect_uri={yourCallbackUrl}&
   scope=openid%20profile&
   code_challenge={codeChallenge}&
   code_challenge_method=S256
   ```

2. **Receive request URI** - Auth0 returns a reference:
   ```json
   {
     "request_uri": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:bwc4JK-ESC0w8acc191e-Y1LTC2",
     "expires_in": 90
   }
   ```

3. **Initiate authorization** - Redirect user to `/authorize` with the request URI:
   ```
   GET https://{yourDomain}/authorize?
     client_id={yourClientId}&
     request_uri=urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:bwc4JK-ESC0w8acc191e-Y1LTC2
   ```

4. **Complete flow** - Continue with standard authorization code exchange.

**Benefits:** Keeps sensitive parameters out of browser history and prevents parameter tampering.

**Learn more:** [Pushed Authorization Requests](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/authorization-code-flow/authorization-code-flow-with-par)

---

## Token Types

### Access Token
- **Purpose:** Access protected APIs
- **Format:** JWT (JSON Web Token) or opaque string
- **Lifetime:** Typically 24 hours (configurable)
- **Contains:** User permissions (scopes), audience (API identifier)

### ID Token
- **Purpose:** Contains user profile information
- **Format:** Always JWT
- **Contains:** User claims (name, email, etc.), issuer, expiration
- **Use:** Client-side user information display

### Refresh Token
- **Purpose:** Obtain new access tokens
- **Format:** Opaque string
- **Lifetime:** Long-lived (days to months)
- **Security:** Must be stored securely, can be revoked

**Learn more:** [Tokens](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/tokens)

---

## Authentication Methods

You have multiple options for authenticating with this API:

### 1. OAuth2 Access Token

Send a valid Access Token in the `Authorization` header using the `Bearer` scheme:

```
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
```

**Example:** The `/userinfo` endpoint requires a valid access token.

### 2. Client ID and Client Secret

Send Client ID and Client Secret in the request. The method depends on your application's Token Endpoint Authentication Method:

**HTTP Basic Authentication:**
```
Authorization: Basic BASE64(client_id:client_secret)
```

**POST Body:**
```
client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&client_secret=YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET
```

### 3. Client ID and Client Assertion (Private Key JWT)

For enhanced security, use a signed JWT:

```
client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
client_assertion_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer&
client_assertion=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
```

**Learn more:** [Private Key JWT](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/authenticate-with-private-key-jwt)

### 4. Client ID Only

For public applications (SPAs, mobile apps) that cannot securely store secrets:

```
client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID
```

### 5. mTLS Authentication

For highly regulated industries, use mutual TLS:

- Client presents certificate during TLS handshake
- Certificate verified by Auth0 edge network
- No client secret needed

**Learn more:** [mTLS Authentication](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/authentication-and-authorization-flow/authenticate-with-mtls)

---

## Request Parameters

### GET Requests

Parameters are passed as query strings:

```
GET https://{yourDomain}/authorize?client_id=abc123&response_type=code&redirect_uri=https://app.com/callback
```

### POST Requests

Parameters are encoded as JSON or form data:

**JSON:**
```
POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "grant_type": "authorization_code",
  "code": "abc123",
  "client_id": "YOUR_CLIENT_ID"
}
```

**Form-encoded:**
```
POST https://{yourDomain}/oauth/token
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=authorization_code&code=abc123&client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID
```

---

## Testing

You can test the endpoints using the [Authentication API Debugger](https://auth0.com/docs/customize/extensions/authentication-api-debugger-extension) extension.

---

## Errors

When an error occurs, you will receive an error object with an error code and description:

```json
{
  "error": "invalid_request",
  "error_description": "The request is missing a required parameter: redirect_uri"
}
```

### HTTP Status Codes

- **4xx** - Client errors (bad request, invalid parameters, unauthorized)
- **5xx** - Server errors (check [Auth0 Status Page](https://status.auth0.com/))

**Common errors:**
- `invalid_request` - Missing or invalid parameters
- `invalid_client` - Invalid client credentials
- `invalid_grant` - Invalid authorization code or refresh token
- `unauthorized_client` - Client not authorized for this grant type
- `access_denied` - User denied authorization
- `mfa_required` - Multi-factor authentication required

---

## Rate Limiting

The Authentication API is subject to rate limiting. Limits vary by endpoint.

If you exceed the rate limit, you will receive:

```
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
X-RateLimit-Limit: 10
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1640000000

{
  "error": "too_many_requests",
  "error_description": "Too many requests. Check the X-RateLimit-* headers."
}
```

### Rate Limit Headers

- `X-RateLimit-Limit` - Requests allowed per time window
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining` - Requests remaining in current window
- `X-RateLimit-Reset` - UTC epoch seconds when limit resets

**Learn more:** [Rate Limit Policy](https://auth0.com/docs/troubleshoot/customer-support/operational-policies/rate-limit-policy)

---

## Legacy Endpoints

This API includes legacy endpoints marked with **[Legacy]** tags and the `deprecated` flag. These endpoints are part of the legacy authentication pipeline and are maintained for backward compatibility only.

**For new implementations, use the current endpoints documented above.**

Legacy endpoints may:
- Be disabled for new tenants
- Have limited support
- Not support modern security features
- Be removed in future versions

**Migration Guide:** [OIDC Conformant Authentication](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/login/oidc-conformant-authentication)

---

## Support

If you have problems or need help:

- **Documentation:** [Auth0 Docs](https://auth0.com/docs)
- **Community:** [Auth0 Community](https://community.auth0.com/)
- **Support:** [Support Center](https://support.auth0.com/) (paid plans)

---

## Additional Resources

- [Auth0 Quickstarts](https://auth0.com/docs/quickstarts)
- [SDKs and Libraries](https://auth0.com/docs/libraries)
- [API Authorization](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/apis)
- [Tokens Best Practices](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/tokens/token-best-practices)
- [Security Best Practices](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/security-guidance)

yourDomain.description: Your Auth0 domain (e.g., tenant.auth0.com, tenant.us.auth0.com, tenant.eu.auth0.com)

tags.name: Authorization
tags.description: OAuth 2.0 authorization flows for authenticating users. The `/authorize` endpoint initiates browser-based authentication flows including Authorization Code Flow, Authorization Code Flow with PKCE, and Implicit Flow.

tags.name: Token Exchange
tags.description: Exchange credentials for access tokens, ID tokens, and refresh tokens. The `/oauth/token` endpoint supports multiple OAuth 2.0 grant types including authorization code, refresh token, client credentials, password, device code, and Auth0-specific MFA and passwordless grants.

tags.name: Multi-factor Authentication
tags.description: MFA enrollment, challenge request, and verification endpoints. Use these endpoints to implement multi-factor authentication using OTP (authenticator apps), OOB (SMS/push), or recovery codes.

tags.name: Passwordless
tags.description: Passwordless authentication via email or SMS. Users can log in using a one-time code or magic link sent to their email or phone number.

tags.name: User Profile
tags.description: Retrieve authenticated user information using the OpenID Connect UserInfo endpoint.

tags.name: Logout
tags.description: End user sessions and log users out of Auth0 and optionally their identity provider. Supports Auth0 logout, OIDC RP-Initiated Logout, SAML logout, and universal logout.

tags.name: Device Authorization
tags.description: OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Flow for input-constrained devices like smart TVs, IoT devices, and gaming consoles.

tags.name: Token Management
tags.description: Refresh access tokens and revoke refresh tokens.

tags.name: Pushed Authorization Requests
tags.description: Push authorization request parameters to Auth0 before initiating the authorization flow. Used for high-security scenarios and FAPI compliance.

tags.name: Database Connections
tags.description: User signup and password management for Auth0 database connections.

tags.name: Native Social
tags.description: Token exchange for native social authentication (Sign in with Apple, etc.).

tags.name: Dynamic Client Registration
tags.description: Dynamically register OAuth 2.0 clients using OpenID Connect Dynamic Client Registration.

tags.name: SAML
tags.description: SAML protocol endpoints for SAML-based authentication flows.

tags.name: WS-Federation
tags.description: WS-Federation protocol endpoints for legacy enterprise integrations.

tags.name: Login [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: These endpoints are part of the legacy authentication pipeline. Use the current Authorization and Token Exchange endpoints instead.

**Status:** Disabled by default for new tenants as of June 8, 2017.

**Migration:** [OIDC Conformant Authentication](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/login/oidc-conformant-authentication)

tags.name: Resource Owner [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: The `/oauth/ro` endpoint is part of the legacy authentication pipeline.

**Use instead:** `/oauth/token` with `grant_type=password` (Resource Owner Password Flow)

tags.name: User Profile [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: The `/tokeninfo` endpoint is part of the legacy pipeline.

**Use instead:** `/userinfo` endpoint (OpenID Connect UserInfo)

tags.name: Delegation [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: Delegation tokens are deprecated and disabled for new tenants.

**Status:** Maintained only for tenants with active add-ons that require delegation.

**Learn more:** [Delegation Tokens](https://auth0.com/docs/secure/tokens/delegation-tokens)

tags.name: Impersonation [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: User impersonation using Global Client credentials.

**Note:** Global Client credentials are deprecated.

tags.name: Account Linking [Legacy]
tags.description:
⚠️ **LEGACY**: Account linking via the Authentication API is deprecated.

**Use instead:** [Management API v2 - Link User Accounts](https://auth0.com/docs/api/management/v2#!/Users/post_identities)

**Migration:** [Account Linking Migration](https://auth0.com/docs/migrations/past-migrations#account-linking-removal)

get.description:
Initiates the OAuth 2.0 authorization flow by redirecting the user to Auth0's login page. After authentication, Auth0 redirects back to your `redirect_uri` with an authorization code or tokens, depending on the `response_type`.

**Flow context:** This is typically step 1 of a browser-based authentication flow. After receiving the authorization code in the redirect callback, exchange it for tokens at the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) using `grant_type=authorization_code`. If using Pushed Authorization Requests (PAR), call the [PAR Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/pushed-authorization-requests/pushed-authorization-request-par) first to obtain a `request_uri`.

**Supported flows:**
- **Authorization Code Flow** — Use `response_type=code` (server-side applications)
- **Authorization Code Flow with PKCE** — Use `response_type=code` with `code_challenge` and `code_challenge_method=S256` (SPAs and mobile apps)
- **Implicit Flow** — Use `response_type=token` or `response_type=id_token` (deprecated — use PKCE instead)
- **Hybrid Flow** — Use `response_type=code id_token` or `response_type=code token`

parameters.name: response_type
parameters.description:
Specifies the type of response you expect. Determines which OAuth 2.0 flow to use.

- `code` - Authorization Code Flow (returns authorization code)
- `token` - Implicit Flow for Access Token (returns access token)
- `id_token` - Implicit Flow for ID Token (returns ID token)
- `id_token token` - Implicit Flow (returns both)
- `code id_token` - Hybrid Flow
- `code token` - Hybrid Flow
- `code id_token token` - Hybrid Flow

parameters.name: client_id
parameters.description: Your application's Client ID. You can find this value in your [Application Settings](https://manage.auth0.com/#/applications).

parameters.name: redirect_uri
parameters.description:
The URL to which Auth0 will redirect the browser after authorization has been granted by the user. This URL must be registered in your application's **Allowed Callback URLs** settings.

**Note:** Required for most flows, but may be optional if a default is configured.

parameters.name: scope
parameters.description:
Space-delimited list of scopes you want to request authorization for. Include `openid` to get an ID Token. Include `offline_access` to get a Refresh Token.

**OIDC Scopes:**
- `openid` - Required for OpenID Connect
- `profile` - User profile info (name, picture, etc.)
- `email` - User email address
- `address` - User address
- `phone` - User phone number
- `offline_access` - Refresh token

**Custom Scopes:** You can also request custom API scopes defined in your Auth0 APIs.

parameters.name: state
parameters.description:
An opaque arbitrary alphanumeric string your app adds to the initial request that Auth0 includes when redirecting back to your application. This value must be used by the application to prevent CSRF attacks.

**Recommended:** Always include this parameter for security.

parameters.name: audience
parameters.description:
The unique identifier of the target API you want to access. This is the **API Identifier** found in your API settings.

**When to use:** Include this when requesting an Access Token to call a specific API.

parameters.name: code_challenge
parameters.description:
Generated challenge from the `code_verifier` for PKCE flow. Required when using Authorization Code Flow with PKCE.

**Format:** `BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier))`

**PKCE Flow:** Used to prevent authorization code interception attacks in public clients (SPAs, mobile apps).

parameters.name: code_challenge_method
parameters.description:
Method used to generate the challenge. Auth0 supports `S256` (SHA-256).

**Required when:** Using `code_challenge` parameter.

parameters.name: nonce
parameters.description:
A random string value used to associate a client session with an ID Token and to mitigate replay attacks. The nonce value is included in the ID Token.

**Required when:** Using `response_type` that includes `id_token` (Implicit or Hybrid flows).

parameters.name: connection
parameters.description:
The name of the connection configured for your application. Forces the user to sign in with a specific connection.

**Examples:** `google-oauth2`, `facebook`, `Username-Password-Authentication`, `windowslive`, `linkedin`

**When to use:** To skip the Auth0 Universal Login page and go directly to a specific identity provider.

parameters.name: prompt
parameters.description:
Controls the authentication prompts presented to the user.

**Values:**
- `none` - Silent authentication (no prompts, fails if user is not logged in)
- `login` - Forces the user to re-enter credentials
- `consent` - Forces consent prompt even if consent was previously given
- `select_account` - Prompts user to select an account (when multiple sessions exist)

**Use case:** `none` is used for checking if a user is already authenticated without interrupting their experience.

parameters.name: organization
parameters.description:
ID of the organization to use when authenticating a user. When not provided, if your application is configured to **Display Organization Prompt**, the user will be able to enter the organization name when authenticating.

**Use case:** Authenticate users in the context of a specific organization (B2B scenarios).

parameters.name: invitation
parameters.description:
Ticket ID of the organization invitation. When inviting a member to an Organization, your application should handle invitation acceptance by forwarding the invitation parameter along with the organization parameter when the user accepts the invitation.

**Use case:** Organization member invitation acceptance flow.

parameters.name: login_hint
parameters.description:
Hint to the Authorization Server about the login identifier the user might use to log in. Can be an email address or other identifier.

**Use case:** Pre-fill the username/email field on the login page.

parameters.name: acr_values
parameters.description:
Space-separated string that specifies the authentication context class reference values. Used to request specific authentication methods (e.g., MFA).

**Example:** `http://schemas.openid.net/pape/policies/2007/06/multi-factor`

parameters.name: max_age
parameters.description:
Maximum authentication age in seconds. If the elapsed time since the last authentication is greater than this value, the user must re-authenticate.

**Use case:** Require recent authentication for sensitive operations.

parameters.name: ui_locales
parameters.description:
Space-delimited list of locales used to constrain the language list for the login page. The first locale on the list must match an enabled locale in your tenant.

**Example:** `en es fr`

parameters.name: response_mode
parameters.description:
Specifies how the result of the authorization request is formatted.

**Values:**
- `query` - Response parameters encoded in query string (default for code)
- `fragment` - Response parameters encoded in URL fragment (default for token)
- `form_post` - Response parameters sent as form POST to redirect_uri
- `web_message` - For silent authentication in iframes

parameters.name: dpop_jkt
parameters.description:
The JWK Thumbprint of the DPoP proof-of-possession public key using SHA-256 hash function (as defined in RFC 7638).

**Use case:** Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession (DPoP) for enhanced security in OAuth 2.0.

302.description:
Redirect response. Auth0 redirects the user to authenticate or redirects back to your application with the authorization result.

**Authorization Code Flow:** Redirects to `redirect_uri` with `code` and `state` parameters:
```
https://your-app.com/callback?code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE&state=xyzABC123
```

**Implicit Flow (token):** Redirects to `redirect_uri` with tokens in fragment:
```
https://your-app.com/callback#access_token=...&token_type=Bearer&expires_in=86400&state=xyzABC123
```

**Implicit Flow (id_token):** Redirects to `redirect_uri` with ID token in fragment:
```
https://your-app.com/callback#id_token=...&state=xyzABC123
```

Location.description: The URL to which the user is redirected

value.error_description: The request is missing a required parameter: redirect_uri
The authorization server does not support obtaining an authorization code using this method
The user is not logged in
The user denied the authorization request

x-codeSamples.label: Authorization Code Flow
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

x-codeSamples.label: Authorization Code Flow with PKCE
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123&
  code_challenge=E9Melhoa2OwvFrEMTJguCHaoeK1t8URWbuGJSstw-cM&
  code_challenge_method=S256
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

x-codeSamples.label: Implicit Flow (Access Token)
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=token&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123&
  nonce=abc123nonce
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

x-codeSamples.label: Social Connection
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123&
  connection=google-oauth2
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

x-codeSamples.label: Silent Authentication
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123&
  prompt=none
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

x-codeSamples.label: Organization Login
x-codeSamples.source:
GET /authorize?
  response_type=code&
  client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID&
  redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback&
  scope=openid%20profile%20email&
  state=xyzABC123&
  organization=org_abc123
Host: your-tenant.auth0.com

previous.description: Push authorization parameters and obtain request_uri

post.description:
Exchange various types of credentials for access tokens, ID tokens, and refresh tokens. This endpoint is the core of OAuth 2.0 token exchange and supports multiple grant types.

**Flow context:** This endpoint is the token-issuing step in most authentication flows. It is preceded by `/authorize` (Authorization Code and PKCE flows), `/oauth/device/code` (Device Authorization Flow), `/passwordless/start` (Passwordless), `/mfa/challenge` (MFA verification), `/bc-authorize` (CIBA), or called directly (Client Credentials, Resource Owner Password, Refresh Token). If the response is an `mfa_required` error, continue to the [MFA Challenge](/docs/api/authentication/multi-factor-authentication/request-mfa-challenge) endpoint.

**Supported grant types:**
- **authorization_code** — Exchange authorization code for tokens (Authorization Code Flow)
- **authorization_code** + code_verifier — Exchange with PKCE (Authorization Code Flow with PKCE)
- **refresh_token** — Get new access token using refresh token
- **client_credentials** — Machine-to-machine authentication
- **password** — Resource Owner Password flow
- **urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code** — Device authorization flow
- **http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/mfa-otp** — MFA verification with OTP
- **http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/mfa-oob** — MFA verification with OOB
- **http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/mfa-recovery-code** — MFA verification with recovery code
- **http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/passwordless/otp** — Passwordless authentication
- **urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange** — Token exchange for native social

The request body parameters vary based on the grant_type. See the examples below for each flow.

parameters.name: DPoP
parameters.description: A DPoP proof JWT for enhanced security. Required only if your application uses Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession (DPoP).

authorizationCode.description: Exchange an authorization code for tokens. Used by server-side web applications that can securely store a client secret.

pkce.description: Exchange an authorization code for tokens using PKCE. Used by SPAs and mobile apps that cannot securely store a client secret.

refreshToken.description: Obtain a new access token using a refresh token without requiring user interaction.

clientCredentials.description: Machine-to-machine authentication. Request an access token using application credentials without user involvement.

password.description: Exchange user credentials for tokens. Only use this for highly trusted first-party applications.

deviceCode.description: Exchange a device code for tokens. Used by input-constrained devices like smart TVs.

mfaOtp.description: Complete multi-factor authentication using a one-time password from an authenticator app.

mfaOob.description: Complete multi-factor authentication using out-of-band verification (SMS, push notification).

mfaRecoveryCode.description: Complete multi-factor authentication using a recovery code when the primary MFA method is unavailable.

passwordlessOtp.description: Authenticate using a one-time code sent via email or SMS (passwordless authentication).

tokenExchange.description: Exchange a native social provider token (e.g., Sign in with Apple) for Auth0 tokens.

200.description: Successful token exchange. Returns access token and optionally ID token and refresh token.

DPoP-Nonce.description: A DPoP nonce that must be included in the DPoP proof as the 'nonce' claim for subsequent requests to this endpoint.

withRefreshToken.description: Typical response for Authorization Code Flow with offline_access scope

withoutRefreshToken.description: Response when offline_access scope was not requested

clientCredentials.description: Machine-to-machine flow returns only access token (no ID token or refresh token)

RevokeRefreshToken.description: Revoke the refresh token when it is no longer needed.

GetUserProfile.description: Retrieve the authenticated user's profile using the access token.

400.description: Bad Request - Invalid parameters or grant

DPoP-Nonce.description: A DPoP nonce for subsequent requests

mfaRequired.description: Multi-factor authentication is required. Use the mfa_token to complete MFA challenge.

403.description: Forbidden - Access denied or invalid credentials

RequestMfaChallenge.description: Request an MFA challenge using the mfa_token from this error response.

429.description: Too Many Requests - Rate limit exceeded

value.error_description: Rate limit exceeded. Please retry after some time.

previous.description: User authenticates and receives authorization code
User authenticates and receives authorization code
Obtain device code and user code
Send verification code or magic link

post.description:
Request a challenge for multi-factor authentication. Use this endpoint during the MFA flow when a user needs to verify their identity using a second factor.

**Flow context:** This is step 2 of the MFA Verification flow. Called after receiving an `mfa_required` error (with `mfa_token`) from the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint). After receiving the challenge, submit the user's verification code back to the Token Endpoint with the appropriate MFA grant type (`mfa-otp` or `mfa-oob`).

**Challenge types:**
- `otp` — One-time password from authenticator app
- `oob` — Out-of-band via SMS, voice, or push notification

mfa_token.description: The MFA token received in the mfa_required error response from /oauth/token

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret (required for confidential clients)

challenge_type.description: The type of MFA challenge to request

authenticator_id.description: The ID of a specific authenticator to challenge (optional, for users with multiple MFA methods enrolled)

oob_channel.description: The channel to use for OOB challenges (required when challenge_type is 'oob')

otpChallenge.description: Request a challenge for TOTP authenticator app

oob_code.description: The OOB code to use when completing the challenge (for OOB challenges)

binding_method.description: The binding method for the OOB challenge

CompleteMfaWithOtp.description: Complete MFA by submitting the OTP code to the token endpoint.

CompleteMfaWithOob.description: Complete MFA by submitting the OOB code to the token endpoint.

previous.description: Initial token request returned mfa_required error

next.description: Submit MFA code to complete authentication

post.description:
Associate a new MFA authenticator with the user's account. Use this endpoint to enroll users in multi-factor authentication.

**Flow context:** This is the enrollment step for MFA. After calling this endpoint, display the QR code or secret to the user (for OTP), then have them enter the first code to confirm enrollment. Use the [List MFA Authenticators](/docs/api/authentication/multi-factor-authentication/list-mfa-authenticators) endpoint to verify enrollment.

**Supported authenticator types:**
- `otp` — TOTP authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, etc.)
- `oob` — Out-of-band (SMS, voice)

authenticator_types.description: List of authenticator types to enroll

oob_channels.description: List of OOB channels (required when enrolling OOB authenticators)

phone_number.description: Phone number for SMS/voice OOB (required when enrolling SMS/voice)

secret.description: The secret key for OTP authenticators

barcode_uri.description: QR code URI for OTP authenticators

ListAuthenticators.description: List all enrolled authenticators to verify enrollment status.

401.description: Unauthorized - Invalid or missing access token

get.description:
List all MFA authenticators enrolled on the authenticated user's account. Returns authenticator type, active status, and channel information for each enrolled factor.

**Flow context:** Use this endpoint during MFA flows to determine which authenticators are available before requesting a challenge via the [Request MFA Challenge](/docs/api/authentication/multi-factor-authentication/request-mfa-challenge) endpoint. Also useful for building authenticator management UIs.

**Note:** Requires a valid access token with MFA permissions. The token is typically the `mfa_token` returned from an `mfa_required` error, or an access token with `enroll` scope for enrollment flows.

delete.description:
Delete a specific MFA authenticator from the user's account.

**Flow context:** Use this endpoint to remove an enrolled authenticator from a user's account. Typically called from an authenticator management UI after listing authenticators via the [List MFA Authenticators](/docs/api/authentication/multi-factor-authentication/list-mfa-authenticators) endpoint.

**Note:** Requires a valid access token with MFA permissions. Users must have at least one authenticator remaining or MFA will be disabled for their account.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The ID of the authenticator to delete

post.description:
Start a passwordless authentication flow by sending a verification code or magic link to the user's email or phone.

**Flow context:** This is step 1 of the Passwordless Authentication flow. After the user receives and enters the verification code, exchange it for tokens by calling the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) with `grant_type=http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/passwordless/otp`.

**Supported connection types:**
- `email` — Send a code or magic link via email
- `sms` — Send a one-time code via SMS

**Send options:**
- `code` — Send a one-time verification code (email or SMS)
- `link` — Send a magic link (email only)

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret (required for confidential clients)

connection.description: The connection type for passwordless authentication

email.description: The user's email address (required when connection is 'email')

phone_number.description: The user's phone number in E.164 format (required when connection is 'sms')

send.description: Whether to send a magic link or a code. Only 'code' is supported for SMS.

authParams.description: Additional authentication parameters to include (scope, state, etc.)

ExchangePasswordlessCode.description: After the user receives the code, exchange it for tokens.

post.description:
Request a device code and user code for the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Flow. This flow is designed for input-constrained devices like smart TVs, IoT devices, or gaming consoles.

**Flow context:** This is step 1 of the Device Authorization Flow. After receiving the response:
1. Display the `user_code` and `verification_uri` to the user
2. The user visits the URL on another device and enters the code
3. Your device polls the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) with `grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code` using the `device_code` at the specified `interval` until the user completes authorization

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

device_code.description: The device verification code used when polling for tokens

user_code.description: The end-user verification code. Display this to the user.

verification_uri.description: The URI the user should visit to enter the user_code

verification_uri_complete.description: Optional URI that includes the user_code for easier user experience

expires_in.description: The lifetime in seconds of the device_code and user_code

interval.description: The minimum number of seconds to wait between polling requests to /oauth/token

PollForTokens.description: Poll this endpoint with the device_code until the user completes authentication.

next.description: Poll for tokens using the device_code

post.description:
Revoke a refresh token to prevent it from being used to obtain new access tokens. Once revoked, the refresh token can no longer be used.

**Flow context:** Call this endpoint when a user logs out, when there are security concerns (token compromise), or when the user revokes application access. Typically called after tokens have been issued by the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint).

**Note:** This endpoint only revokes refresh tokens. Access tokens cannot be revoked and will remain valid until they expire.

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret (required for confidential clients)

token.description: The refresh token you want to revoke

client_assertion.description: A JWT signed with your client secret or private key (for Private Key JWT authentication)

200.description: Token revoked successfully (or token was already invalid/revoked)

externalDocs.description: Learn more about Revoking Refresh Tokens

post.description:
Push an authorization request to Auth0 and receive a request URI to use in the authorization flow. PAR enhances security by moving authorization parameters from the front channel (browser) to the back channel (direct server-to-server).

**Flow context:** This is step 1 of the Pushed Authorization Request (PAR) flow. After receiving the `request_uri`, redirect the user to the [Authorization Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/authorization/authorization-endpoint) with only `client_id` and `request_uri` as parameters. Auth0 retrieves the full authorization parameters using the `request_uri`.

**Benefits:**
- Prevents parameter tampering
- Keeps sensitive data out of browser history
- Required for FAPI (Financial-grade API) compliance
- Supports large authorization requests

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret (required for confidential clients)

redirect_uri.description: The redirect URI for your application

state.description: An opaque value to maintain state between request and callback

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API

code_challenge_method.description: The method used to generate the code challenge

nonce.description: A random string value for ID Token validation

client_assertion.description: A JWT signed with your client secret or private key (for Private Key JWT authentication)

request_uri.description: The request URI to use in the authorization request. Pass this to the /authorize endpoint.

expires_in.description: The lifetime in seconds of the request URI (typically 90 seconds)

InitiateAuthorization.description: Redirect the user to /authorize with the request_uri from this response.

next.description: Redirect user to /authorize with the request_uri

get.description:
Returns profile claims about the authenticated user per the OpenID Connect UserInfo specification.

**Flow context:** Call this endpoint after completing any authentication flow that issues an access token with the `openid` scope (e.g., Authorization Code, PKCE, Device Authorization). Send the access token as a Bearer token in the `Authorization` header.

**Returned claims depend on:**
- The `scope` requested during authentication (`profile`, `email`, `address`, `phone`)
- Custom claims added via Rules or Actions
- The API (audience) the access token was issued for

profile.description: URL of the user's profile page

picture.description: URL of the user's profile picture

email_verified.description: Whether the user's email address has been verified

phone_number_verified.description: Whether the user's phone number has been verified

updated_at.description: Time the user's information was last updated

401.description: Unauthorized - Invalid or missing access token

example.error_description: The access token is invalid or has expired

get.description:
Log out a user and optionally redirect them to a specified URL. This endpoint clears the user's session with Auth0.

**Flow context:** Call this endpoint when ending a user's session. Typically invoked after the user clicks a "Log out" button in your application. For OIDC-compliant logout, use the [OIDC Logout Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/logout/oidc-rp-initiated-logout) instead.

**Logout types:**
- **Local logout** — Clears the Auth0 session only (no `federated` parameter)
- **Federated logout** — Also logs out from the upstream identity provider (pass `federated` parameter)

**Note:** The `returnTo` URL must be registered in your application's **Allowed Logout URLs** settings.

parameters.name: returnTo
parameters.description: URL to redirect the user after logout. Must be registered in Allowed Logout URLs.

parameters.name: client_id
parameters.description: Your application's Client ID. Required if `returnTo` is specified.

parameters.name: federated
parameters.description: Add this parameter (with any value) to log the user out of their identity provider as well.

302.description: Redirect to returnTo URL or Auth0 logout confirmation page

Location.description: The URL to which the user is redirected

get.description:
OpenID Connect RP-Initiated Logout endpoint. This is the standardized OIDC logout endpoint that allows relying parties (your application) to initiate logout.

**Flow context:** Use this endpoint for OIDC-compliant logout. It follows the [RP-Initiated Logout specification](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html). For Auth0-specific logout with the `returnTo` parameter, use the [Logout Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/logout/logout) (`/v2/logout`) instead.

**Differences from /v2/logout:**
- Follows OIDC RP-Initiated Logout specification
- Uses `post_logout_redirect_uri` instead of `returnTo`
- Uses `id_token_hint` for identifying the session

**Note:** The `post_logout_redirect_uri` must be registered in your application's **Allowed Logout URLs** settings.

parameters.name: id_token_hint
parameters.description: The ID Token obtained during authentication. Provides a hint about the user's session to be terminated.

parameters.name: post_logout_redirect_uri
parameters.description: URL to redirect the user after logout. Must be registered in Allowed Logout URLs.

parameters.name: state
parameters.description: Opaque value to maintain state between logout request and callback

parameters.name: client_id
parameters.description: Your application's Client ID

parameters.name: federated
parameters.description: Add this parameter to also log the user out of their identity provider

302.description: Redirect to post_logout_redirect_uri or logout confirmation page

Location.description: The URL to which the user is redirected

post.description:
Create a new user in an Auth0 database connection.

**Flow context:** This endpoint only creates the user account. After signup, redirect the user to the [Authorization Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/authorization/authorization-endpoint) to authenticate and receive tokens. Alternatively, use `/oauth/token` with `grant_type=password` for trusted first-party applications.

**Note:** The user must meet the connection's password strength requirements. The response includes the user's `_id` and `email` but does not issue tokens.

password.description: The user's desired password. Must meet the connection's password strength requirements.

connection.description: The name of the database connection (e.g., 'Username-Password-Authentication')

username.description: The user's desired username (if the connection requires usernames)

picture.description: URL of the user's profile picture

email_verified.description: Whether the email has been verified

AuthenticateUser.description: Redirect the user to authenticate after account creation.

400.description: Bad Request - Invalid parameters or password doesn't meet requirements

post.description:
Send a password reset email to a user. The user will receive an email with a link to reset their password.

**Flow context:** This is a self-service password reset flow:
1. User requests password reset via this endpoint
2. Auth0 sends email with reset link
3. User clicks link and is redirected to change password page
4. User sets new password

**Note:** This endpoint only initiates the password reset flow. The actual password change happens when the user follows the link in the email. The response is always the same regardless of whether the user exists, to prevent email enumeration.

connection.description: The name of the database connection

organization.description: The ID of the organization (for organization-specific password reset)

200.description: Password reset email sent successfully (or user not found - response is the same for security)

text/plain.example: We've just sent you an email to reset your password.

post.description:
**Deprecated:** This endpoint is part of the legacy authentication pipeline and is deprecated. Use the standard [Authorize Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/login/authorize) with a social connection instead.

Given the social provider's access token and the connection, this endpoint authenticates the user with the provider and returns a JSON with the access token and, optionally, an ID token. Only works for Facebook, Google, Twitter, and Weibo.

**Status:** Disabled by default for new tenants as of June 8, 2017.

**Migration:** Use browser-based social authentication instead, which is what [Google and Facebook recommend](https://developers.googleblog.com/2016/08/modernizing-oauth-interactions-in-native-apps.html). Use the standard `/authorize` endpoint with a social connection.

connection.description: The name of the social connection

post.description:
**Deprecated:** This endpoint is part of the legacy authentication pipeline and is deprecated. Use the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) with `grant_type=password` instead.

This endpoint allows you to authenticate a user using their username and password.

**Status:** Part of legacy pipeline. New applications should not use this endpoint.

**Migration:** Use `/oauth/token` with `grant_type=password` (Resource Owner Password Flow) or preferably use redirect-based flows like Authorization Code Flow with PKCE.

connection.description: The name of the database connection

post.description:
**Deprecated:** This endpoint is deprecated. Use the [UserInfo Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/user-profile/user-info) instead.

Validates an ID Token and returns the user profile information.

**Status:** Part of legacy pipeline. Returns limited user profile information.

**Migration:** Use the `/userinfo` endpoint (OpenID Connect UserInfo) instead, which is the standard OIDC endpoint for retrieving user information.

id_token.description: The ID Token to validate and extract user information from

200.description: Token is valid, returns user information

email_verified.description: Whether the email has been verified

picture.description: URL of the user's profile picture

post.description:
**Deprecated:** Delegation tokens are deprecated and disabled for new tenants. There is no direct replacement.

Allowed applications to obtain an ID Token or a JWT for calling a different API using an existing token.

**Status:** This feature is disabled by default for new tenants. Maintained only for tenants with active add-ons that require delegation.

**Migration:** There is no direct replacement. Specific use cases should migrate to:
- Token refresh: Use refresh tokens with `/oauth/token` and `grant_type=refresh_token`
- Third-party APIs: Configure proper API authorization

id_token.description: The ID Token you want to exchange

target.description: The Client ID of the target application

post.description:
**Deprecated:** User impersonation using Global Client credentials is deprecated. Use the [Management API v2](/docs/api/management/v2) for impersonation flows instead.

Generates an impersonation URL that logs you in as another user. Useful for customer support scenarios.

**Status:** This endpoint uses Global Client credentials which are deprecated.

**Migration:** Use the Management API v2 for impersonation flows with proper client credentials.

parameters.name: user_id
parameters.description: The ID of the user to impersonate

protocol.description: The protocol to use for impersonation

impersonator_id.description: The ID of the user performing the impersonation (admin)

client_id.description: The Client ID of the application to impersonate into

post.description:
Initiates a Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication (CIBA) request, sending a push notification to the user's enrolled device via the Guardian SDK. The user approves or rejects the request on their device while your application polls for tokens.

**Flow context:** This is step 1 of the Back-Channel Authentication (CIBA) flow. After receiving the `auth_req_id`, poll the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) with `grant_type=urn:openid:params:grant-type:ciba` at the interval specified in the response until the user approves.

**Use cases:**
- Users not in front of the requesting application (e.g., call center scenarios)
- Consumption device is insecure for sensitive operations (e.g., web browser for financial transactions)
- Device has limited interactive capability (e.g., e-bicycles, kiosks)

**Note:** The `login_hint` value must follow the `iss_sub` format: `{ "format": "iss_sub", "iss": "https://[TENANT_DOMAIN]/", "sub": "auth0|[USER_ID]" }`. Users must have a device enrolled for push notifications using the Guardian SDK.

binding_message.description: A human-readable string for the user to ensure they approve the correct request.

login_hint.description: A JSON object containing user details for authentication in the `iss_sub` format.

scope.description: Space-separated list of OIDC and custom API scopes, including `openid` at a minimum.

audience.description: Optional unique identifier of the target API for access tokens.

resource.description: The identifier of the target API (resource server) you want to access. Must match an API Identifier registered in your Auth0 tenant. Used as an alternative to `audience` when the tenant's [Resource Parameter Compatibility Profile](https://auth0.com/docs/get-started/tenant-settings#settings-advanced) is set to `compatibility`.

requested_expiry.description: Custom expiry time for the request in seconds (1-300). Defaults to 300 seconds.

authorization_details.description: An optional JSON array of objects that describe the permissions to be authorized. Each object’s `type` value should be previously registered on the resource server using the Resource Server’s `authorization_details` parameter. To learn more, read the [Update a resource server](https://auth0.com/docs/api/management/v2/resource-servers/patch-resource-servers-by-id#body-parameters) Management API documentation.

PollForTokens.description: Poll for tokens using the auth_req_id until the user approves on their device.

next.description: Poll for tokens until user approves on their device

externalDocs.description: Learn more about Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication (CIBA) Flow

post.description:
**Deprecated:** This endpoint is disabled by default for new tenants as of June 8, 2017. Use the [Token Endpoint](/docs/api/authentication/token-exchange/token-endpoint) with `grant_type=http://auth0.com/oauth/grant-type/passwordless/otp` instead.

Verifies a passwordless authentication code and logs the user in. After receiving a verification code via `/passwordless/start`, use this endpoint to authenticate the user with their phone number or email and the verification code.

**Note:** The `profile` scope requests access to the End-User's default profile claims: `name`, `family_name`, `given_name`, `middle_name`, `nickname`, `preferred_username`, `profile`, `picture`, `website`, `gender`, `birthdate`, `zoneinfo`, `locale`, and `updated_at`. The `email` scope requests access to `email` and `email_verified` claims.

connection.description: Use `sms` or `email` (should be the same as [POST /passwordless/start](#get-code-or-link)).

username.description: The user's phone number if `connection=sms`, or the user's email if `connection=email`.

scope.description: Use `openid` to get an ID Token, or `openid profile email` to include user profile information in the ID Token.

redirect_uri.description: Callback URL registered with your application's Allowed Callback URLs.

post.description:
Dynamically register a new OAuth 2.0 client application with Auth0, following the OpenID Connect Dynamic Client Registration specification. No authentication token is required.

**Note:** The registered application receives a `client_id` and `client_secret` immediately upon successful registration. The `redirect_uris` provided must be valid callback URLs for the application.

client_name.description: The name of the Dynamic Client to be created.

redirect_uris.description: An array of URLs that Auth0 will deem valid to call at the end of an Authentication flow.

token_endpoint_auth_method.description: Method used for authentication at the token endpoint.

client_name.description: The name of the newly registered client.

client_id.description: The unique identifier for the client.

client_secret.description: The secret key for the client.

redirect_uris.description: The valid redirect URIs for the client.

client_secret_expires_at.description: Expiration time for the client secret.

externalDocs.description: Learn more about Dynamic Client Registration

get.description:
Accept a SAML request to initiate a login. Use this endpoint when Auth0 is configured as a SAML Identity Provider (IdP) for your application.

**Note:** Optionally include a `connection` parameter to log in with a specific provider. If no connection is specified, the Auth0 Universal Login page will be shown. SP-initiated login requests can include an `organization` parameter to authenticate users in the context of an organization.

parameters.name: clientId
parameters.description: Client ID of your application.

organization.description: Organization ID, if authenticating in the context of an organization.

get.description:
Returns the SAML 2.0 metadata XML document for the specified application. Use this metadata to configure your Service Provider (SP) with Auth0 as the Identity Provider (IdP).

**Note:** The metadata includes the IdP's entity ID, SSO endpoint URL, SLO endpoint URL, and the X.509 signing certificate.

parameters.name: clientId
parameters.description: The client_id of your application.

post.description:
Log out a user from an Auth0 tenant configured as a SAML identity provider (IdP). Logout behavior is determined by the configuration of the SAML2 Web App addon for the application.

**Note:** The POST body must contain a valid SAML `<LogoutRequest>` message per the [SAML V2.0 specification](https://docs.oasis-open.org/security/saml/v2.0/saml-core-2.0-os.pdf).

parameters.name: client_id
parameters.description: Client ID of your application configured with the [SAML2 Web App addon](https://auth0.com/docs/authenticate/protocols/saml/saml-sso-integrations/enable-saml2-web-app-addon).

schema.description: SAML `<LogoutRequest>` message to be sent as the body of the request.

400.description: Bad request, possibly due to an invalid SAML message

externalDocs.description: Learn more about SAML IdP Logout

get.description:
Accept a WS-Federation request to initiate a login.

**Note:** The `wtrealm` parameter must be in one of these formats: `urn:clientID` (e.g., `urn:YOUR_CLIENT_ID`) or a value matching the `client.clientAliases` array (configurable via the [Management API](https://auth0.com/docs/api/management/v2/clients/get-clients)). The `whr` parameter maps to a connection as `urn:CONNECTION_NAME` (e.g., `urn:google-oauth2`). If `whr` is not included, the user will be directed to the Auth0 Login Page.

parameters.name: client-id
parameters.description: The `client-id` of your application.

parameters.name: wtrealm
parameters.description: Can be used in place of `client-id`.

parameters.name: whr
parameters.description: The name of the connection (used to skip the login page).

parameters.name: wctx
parameters.description: Your application's state.

parameters.name: wreply
parameters.description: The callback URL.

get.description:
Returns the WS-Federation metadata XML document. Use this metadata to configure your relying party application for WS-Federation authentication with Auth0.

**Note:** The metadata follows the WS-Federation Metadata 1.0 specification.

post.description:
Accepts an IdP-Initiated Sign On SAMLResponse from a SAML Identity Provider. The connection corresponding to the identity provider is specified in the query string.

**Flow context:** This endpoint is the callback target for IdP-initiated SAML SSO flows. The Identity Provider sends a SAMLResponse directly to this endpoint without a prior AuthnRequest. The user will be redirected to the application specified in the SAML Provider IdP-Initiated Sign On configuration.

connection.description: The name of an identity provider configured to your application.

SAMLResponse.description: An IdP-Initiated Sign On SAML Response.

400.description: Bad request due to missing or invalid parameters.

post.description:
**Deprecated:** This endpoint is deprecated. Use [DELETE /api/v2/users/{id}/identities/{provider}/{user_id}](https://auth0.com/docs/api/management/v2#!/Users/delete_user_identity_by_user_id) instead.

Unlinks a user's account from an identity provider using their access token and user ID.

**Migration:** Use the Management API v2 [Unlink User Accounts](https://auth0.com/docs/manage-users/user-accounts/user-account-linking/unlink-user-accounts) endpoint.

externalDocs.description: Learn more about Unlinking User Accounts

post.description:
Revoke session cookies and refresh tokens for a user across all applications connected via a specific connection. Use this endpoint with the Okta Workforce Identity Cloud Universal Logout to log users out of your applications.

**Flow context:** This endpoint is used for enterprise-wide session termination. It is typically called by an upstream Identity Provider (e.g., Okta Workforce) when a user's access needs to be revoked across all connected applications.

**Note:** This endpoint revokes session cookies and refresh tokens but not access tokens. The request must be authenticated before revoking user sessions.

parameters.name: connection_name
parameters.description: The name of the connection for which you want to revoke the user's session.

iss.description: The issuer URL (typically your authorization server's URL).

sub.description: The subject identifier (typically the user identifier).

200.description: Successfully revoked session cookies and refresh tokens.

400.description: Bad request, possibly due to an invalid `subject` format or missing authentication.

bearerAuth.description: Access token obtained from the authentication flow

error.description: Error code indicating the type of error that occurred

error_description.description: Human-readable text providing additional information about the error
error_description.example: The request is missing a required parameter

error_uri.description: URI identifying a human-readable web page with information about the error

TokenResponse.description: Successful token response containing access token and optionally ID token and refresh token

access_token.description: The access token issued by the authorization server. Use this token to access protected resources (APIs).

token_type.description: The type of the token issued. Always 'Bearer' for Auth0.

expires_in.description: The lifetime in seconds of the access token. After this time, the token will expire and can no longer be used.

refresh_token.description: The refresh token which can be used to obtain new access tokens. Only returned if 'offline_access' scope was requested.

id_token.description: The ID Token containing user profile information. Only returned if 'openid' scope was requested. This is a JWT containing user claims.

scope.description: The scopes granted by the authorization server. May differ from requested scopes.

DPoPHeader.description: DPoP (Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession) header parameters for enhanced OAuth 2.0 security

DPoP.description: A DPoP proof JWT. This is a signed JWT that proves possession of a private key corresponding to a public key.

DPoPNonceHeader.description: DPoP nonce header returned by the server

DPoP-Nonce.description: A server-generated nonce that must be included in the DPoP proof as the 'nonce' claim for subsequent requests. Helps prevent replay attacks.

AuthorizationCodeGrant.description: Authorization Code Grant - Exchange an authorization code for tokens. Used by server-side web applications that can securely store a client secret.
AuthorizationCodeGrant.title: Authorization Code

code.description: The authorization code received from the authorization endpoint

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

redirect_uri.description: The same redirect_uri used in the authorization request. Must match exactly.

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

client_assertion.description: A JWT signed with your client secret or private key (for Private Key JWT authentication)

AuthorizationCodePKCEGrant.description: Authorization Code Grant with PKCE - Exchange an authorization code for tokens using PKCE. Used by SPAs and mobile apps that cannot securely store a client secret.
AuthorizationCodePKCEGrant.title: Authorization Code (PKCE)

code.description: The authorization code received from the authorization endpoint

code_verifier.description: The original code verifier for the PKCE request. The authorization server will hash this and compare it to the code_challenge from the authorization request.

redirect_uri.description: The same redirect_uri used in the authorization request. Must match exactly.

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

RefreshTokenGrant.description: Refresh Token Grant - Obtain a new access token using a refresh token without requiring user interaction.
RefreshTokenGrant.title: Refresh Token

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

refresh_token.description: The refresh token returned from the initial authorization

scope.description: Space-delimited list of scopes. Requested scopes must not exceed the scopes granted in the original access token.

client_assertion.description: A JWT signed with your client secret or private key (for Private Key JWT authentication)

ClientCredentialsGrant.description: Client Credentials Grant - Machine-to-machine authentication. Request an access token using application credentials without user involvement.
ClientCredentialsGrant.title: Client Credentials

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access. Required for Client Credentials flow.

organization.description: The ID of the organization to authenticate against

client_assertion.description: A JWT signed with your client secret or private key (for Private Key JWT authentication)

PasswordGrant.description: Resource Owner Password Grant - Exchange user credentials for tokens. Only use this for highly trusted first-party applications.
PasswordGrant.title: Resource Owner Password

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

username.description: The user's username or email address

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

realm.description: The realm the user belongs to (name of the database connection)

DeviceCodeGrant.description: Device Authorization Grant - Exchange a device code for tokens. Used by input-constrained devices like smart TVs.
DeviceCodeGrant.title: Device Authorization

device_code.description: The device code received from the /oauth/device/code endpoint

MfaOtpGrant.description: MFA OTP Grant - Complete multi-factor authentication using a one-time password from an authenticator app.
MfaOtpGrant.title: MFA with OTP

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

mfa_token.description: The MFA token received in the mfa_required error response

otp.description: The one-time password from the user's authenticator app

MfaOobGrant.description: MFA OOB Grant - Complete multi-factor authentication using out-of-band verification (SMS, push notification).
MfaOobGrant.title: MFA with OOB

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

mfa_token.description: The MFA token received in the mfa_required error response

oob_code.description: The OOB code received from the /mfa/challenge endpoint

binding_code.description: The binding code displayed to the user (for SMS/authenticator app verification)

MfaRecoveryCodeGrant.description: MFA Recovery Code Grant - Complete multi-factor authentication using a recovery code when the primary MFA method is unavailable.
MfaRecoveryCodeGrant.title: MFA with Recovery Code

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

mfa_token.description: The MFA token received in the mfa_required error response

recovery_code.description: The recovery code provided to the user during MFA enrollment

PasswordlessOtpGrant.description: Passwordless OTP Grant - Authenticate using a one-time code sent via email or SMS (passwordless authentication).
PasswordlessOtpGrant.title: Passwordless with OTP

client_secret.description: Your application's Client Secret. Required for confidential clients.

username.description: The user's email address or phone number (depending on the connection type)

otp.description: The one-time password sent to the user

realm.description: The connection name used for passwordless authentication (email or sms)

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

TokenExchangeGrant.description: Token Exchange Grant - Exchange a native social provider token (e.g., Sign in with Apple) for Auth0 tokens.
TokenExchangeGrant.title: Token Exchange (Native Social)

subject_token.description: The token from the native social provider (e.g., Apple ID token, Facebook access token)

audience.description: The unique identifier of the target API you want to access

user_profile.description: Optional user profile information from the native provider (JSON string)
